q: In a testcross of a dihybrid, a student records the four offspring phenotype classes and finds that the two most common classes are 'A b' and 'a B'. What does this reveal about the original dihybrid parent?
options: The parent was in coupling, arranged A B / a b
The parent was in repulsion, arranged A b / a B
The genes assort independently, so arrangement cannot be determined
The parent was in coupling, arranged A b / a B
explain: In a testcross the recessive parent contributes only recessive alleles, so offspring phenotypes directly mirror the gametes of the dihybrid parent. The most common classes are the parental (nonrecombinant) gametes, which reflect the parent's two homologs. Most-common 'A b' and 'a B' means the homologs carried A b and a B — the trans (repulsion) configuration, A b / a B.
hint: In a testcross, which offspring classes correspond to the gametes the dihybrid made without recombination, and how do those map onto its two homologs?

q: Genes R and T are 12 map units apart. A plant of genotype R t / r T (repulsion) is testcrossed, producing 500 offspring. Approximately how many offspring are expected in the RT phenotype class?
explain: In repulsion (R t / r T) the parental gametes are Rt and rT, while RT and rt are recombinant. With 12 map units, recombinants total 12% of 500 = 60, split evenly into two classes, so RT gets 30. The parental 88% (440) is split between Rt and rT.
hint: In a trans/repulsion heterozygote, is an RT gamete a parental or a recombinant product?

q: Two researchers study the same two linked genes (5 map units apart) in two different true-breeding crosses. One starts with a coupling dihybrid and the other with a repulsion dihybrid. Which statement is correct?
options: The map distance differs between the two crosses because arrangement changes recombination
The coupling cross has a higher recombination frequency than the repulsion cross
The map distance is 5 m.u. in both; only which phenotypes are most common differs
Recombinant gametes occur only in the coupling arrangement
explain: Recombination frequency between two loci is set by the physical distance separating them, not by which alleles happen to lie on which homolog, so the map distance is 5 m.u. in both crosses. The allelic arrangement only determines which gamete/phenotype combinations are the abundant parental types versus the rare recombinant types: in coupling the two original cis combinations dominate, in repulsion the two original trans combinations dominate. Recombinants arise at the same low frequency in either arrangement.
hint: Does the chance of a crossover occurring at a point between two loci care about which alleles happen to flank it?

q: A testcross of a dihybrid for linked genes D and E yields: 410 D E, 90 D e, 85 d E, 415 d e (total 1000). What is the arrangement of the parent and the map distance?
explain: In a testcross, the two most frequent progeny classes are the parental (nonrecombinant) types, here DE (410) and de (415), indicating the dihybrid parent carried the dominant alleles together: coupling, D E / d e. The recombinant classes are the two smaller ones, De (90) and dE (85), totaling 175. Map distance equals recombinant frequency: 175/1000 = 17.5%, i.e., 17.5 m.u.
hint: Which two progeny classes are most common, and does the map distance use only the recombinant fraction or the total?

q: In a three-point cross with 2,000 total progeny, region I (between genes A and B) has a recombination frequency of 12% and region II (between genes B and C) has a recombination frequency of 9%. How many double-crossover progeny are EXPECTED if there is no interference?
explain: With no interference, the two crossover events are independent, so the expected frequency of double crossovers is the product of the two single-region recombination frequencies: 0.12 × 0.09 = 0.0108. Multiplying by the 2,000 total progeny gives 0.0108 × 2000 = 21.6 expected double-crossover progeny.
hint: If two crossover events are independent, how do you combine their individual probabilities, and what do you multiply that by to get a count out of all progeny?

q: A three-point cross yields an expected 30 double-crossover progeny, but only 18 double crossovers are actually observed. What is the coefficient of coincidence?
explain: The coefficient of coincidence (CoC) is defined as the number of observed double-crossover progeny divided by the number expected: CoC = observed/expected = 18/30 = 0.60. A value below 1 means fewer double crossovers occurred than predicted, reflecting positive interference (the remaining options invert the ratio or miscompute).
hint: Coincidence puts what you actually counted over what you predicted, so check which value belongs on top of the fraction.

q: If the coefficient of coincidence for a three-point cross is 0.75, what is the interference?
explain: Interference (I) is defined as I = 1 - coefficient of coincidence (c.o.c.). With c.o.c. = 0.75, interference = 1 - 0.75 = 0.25. The positive value indicates that crossovers in adjacent regions are partially suppressed relative to the number expected if they occurred independently.
hint: Coincidence measures observed versus expected double crossovers; what fraction is "missing" when the two are normalized to one?

q: A three-point cross shows complete interference. Which statement is true?
options: The coefficient of coincidence equals 1 and interference equals 0
No double-crossover progeny are observed, and the coefficient of coincidence equals 0
Twice as many double crossovers occur as expected
Interference equals 0 and double crossovers occur exactly as predicted
explain: Complete interference (interference = 1) means a crossover in one region totally prevents a crossover in the adjacent region, so no double-crossover progeny are observed. The coefficient of coincidence (c.o.c) is observed DCOs divided by expected DCOs; with zero observed DCOs, c.o.c = 0. Since interference = 1 - c.o.c, interference = 1, confirming that zero double crossovers occur and c.o.c = 0.
hint: If one crossover completely blocks a neighboring crossover, how many double-crossover offspring can actually appear, and what does that make the ratio of observed to expected?

q: In a cross of 1,500 progeny, region I has RF = 0.10 and region II has RF = 0.20. Investigators observe 21 double-crossover individuals. What is the interference?
explain: Expected double crossovers = product of the two recombination frequencies times total progeny = 0.10 × 0.20 × 1500 = 30. The coefficient of coincidence = observed/expected DCO = 21/30 = 0.70. Interference = 1 − coefficient of coincidence = 1 − 0.70 = 0.30.
hint: Interference is one minus the coefficient of coincidence, so be sure you compute the expected double-crossover count first.

q: A geneticist finds that the coefficient of coincidence in a three-point cross is exactly 1.0. What does this indicate?
options: Crossing over did not occur in either region
One crossover strongly suppresses a second nearby crossover
No interference is occurring; double crossovers happen exactly as expected
Double crossovers occur more often than predicted by chance
explain: The coefficient of coincidence equals observed double crossovers divided by expected double crossovers. A value of exactly 1.0 means observed double crossovers equal the number expected if the two crossover events were independent, so interference (1 minus the coefficient) equals 0. This indicates no interference: a crossover in one region neither suppresses nor enhances a crossover in the adjacent region.
hint: Recall how interference is computed from the coefficient of coincidence, and what value of that difference corresponds to events behaving independently.

q: A three-point cross of 800 progeny has interference = 0.40, with region I RF = 0.15 and region II RF = 0.10. How many double-crossover progeny were OBSERVED?
explain: Coefficient of coincidence = 1 - interference = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60. Expected double crossovers = RF_I x RF_II x N = 0.15 x 0.10 x 800 = 12. Observed DCO = c.o.c. x expected = 0.60 x 12 = 7.2, so the observed count is 7.2.
hint: Interference tells you what fraction of expected double crossovers are missing, so what fraction actually shows up?

q: Positive interference (a value between 0 and 1) in a three-point cross means that:
options: More double crossovers occur than would be expected by chance
A crossover in one region reduces the likelihood of a second crossover nearby, so fewer double crossovers occur than expected
The two genes on the outside are completely unlinked
The recombination frequencies of the two regions must be equal
explain: Interference I = 1 - coefficient of coincidence (c.o.c.), where c.o.c. = observed double crossovers / expected double crossovers. Positive interference (I between 0 and 1, c.o.c. below 1) means a crossover in one region reduces the chance of a crossover in the adjacent region, so fewer double crossovers are observed than expected by independent chance. An I above 0 thus reflects suppression of nearby second crossovers, not their enhancement, and says nothing about linkage status or equality of the two recombination frequencies.
hint: If one crossover discourages a neighboring one, will you see more or fewer double crossovers than chance predicts?

q: A researcher performs a chi-square test of independence on testcross data for two genes and obtains a chi-square value far larger than the critical value at p < 0.05. What is the correct conclusion?
options: The testcross must be repeated because chi-square cannot exceed the critical value
explain: The chi-square test of independence on testcross data uses the null hypothesis that the two genes assort independently (expected 1:1:1:1 among progeny). A chi-square far exceeding the critical value at p < 0.05 means the deviation is too large to be due to chance, so the null hypothesis of independent assortment is rejected. In a testcross, rejecting independent assortment indicates the genes are linked on the same chromosome. Chi-square has no upper bound, so it can readily exceed the critical value, and a 9:3:3:1 ratio applies to an F2 dihybrid cross, not a testcross.
hint: Ask what a chi-square larger than the critical value does to the null hypothesis of independent assortment, and what the alternative would then be.

q: For a 2x2 chi-square test of independence comparing two genes in a testcross, how many degrees of freedom are used to find the critical value?
explain: For a chi-square test of independence on an r-by-c contingency table, the degrees of freedom equal (r-1)(c-1). A 2x2 table therefore has (2-1)(2-1) = 1 degree of freedom, independent of the total sample size.
hint: Apply the (r-1)(c-1) rule to a two-row, two-column table.

q: A testcross of 400 progeny is scored for two genes. Under independent assortment, what is the expected number of individuals in each of the four phenotypic classes?
explain: In a testcross the dihybrid parent (AaBb) is crossed to a fully recessive tester (aabb), so progeny phenotypes directly reflect the parent's gametes. Under independent assortment the AaBb parent makes four gamete types (AB, Ab, aB, ab) in equal 1:1:1:1 proportions, yielding four equally frequent phenotypic classes. Dividing the 400 progeny equally gives 400/4 = 100 individuals expected per class.
hint: Into how many equally likely gamete types does a dihybrid sort under independent assortment, and what does dividing the total by that number give?

q: A chi-square test of independence on two-gene testcross data yields a small chi-square value that does not exceed the critical value (p > 0.05). What does this result support?
options: The genes are tightly linked with no recombination
The genes are linked but show high recombination
The chi-square test cannot be applied to these data
explain: In a two-gene testcross, the null hypothesis is independent assortment, which predicts equal (1:1:1:1) frequencies of the four phenotypic classes. A small chi-square value that does not exceed the critical value (p > 0.05) means the observed counts do not deviate significantly from this expectation, so the null is not rejected. This outcome is consistent with the genes being unlinked and assorting independently; linkage (whether tight or with high recombination) would instead skew counts toward parental types and inflate chi-square.
hint: Recall what hypothesis the equal-class expectation represents and what failing to reject it implies.

q: A single crossover occurs between genes M and N during meiosis in a bivalent. Considering all four chromatids that result, what fraction of the products of that meiosis carry a recombinant arrangement of M and N?
explain: A single crossover event physically links and exchanges segments between only 2 of the 4 chromatids in the bivalent; the other 2 chromatids are not involved. This generates exactly 2 recombinant chromatids and leaves 2 parental (nonrecombinant) chromatids, so 2 of the 4 meiotic products from that cell are recombinant. Hence the recombinant fraction is one-half (50%).
hint: Of the four chromatids in the bivalent, how many does a single crossover physically connect, and how many does it never touch?

q: Two genes give a measured recombination frequency of essentially 50% and behave as though independent in a testcross. Which interpretation is consistent with this result?
options: The genes are so close together that no crossovers occur
The genes are tightly linked with strong positive interference
The genes are very far apart on the same chromosome or on different chromosomes
A measurement error occurred, because RF can legitimately exceed 50%
explain: A single crossover involves only two of the four chromatids, so recombination frequency has a hard ceiling of 50%. Genes very far apart on the same chromosome undergo so many crossovers that recombinant and parental gametes occur in roughly equal numbers (RF ~50%), and genes on different chromosomes assort independently (also ~50%). An observed RF at this 50% ceiling is therefore consistent with either of these arrangements and makes the loci appear genetically unlinked. RF cannot legitimately exceed 50%, so an exceedance would indicate error, not biology.
hint: What is the highest value RF can physically reach, and which gene arrangements drive it toward that limit?

q: Why does recombination frequency systematically UNDERESTIMATE the true physical distance between two widely separated genes?
options: Double crossovers between the genes restore the parental combination and go undetected
Single crossovers produce four recombinant chromatids, inflating the count
Crossovers are suppressed near centromeres, so none are scored
RF overcounts recombinants whenever distance exceeds 50 map units
explain: Between two markers, an even number of crossovers (e.g., a double crossover) returns the alleles on the recombinant chromatids to their parental arrangement, so that meiosis is scored as non-recombinant even though physical exchange occurred. Because the chance of these even-numbered, mutually undetected crossovers rises with interval length, the observed recombination frequency falls below the true amount of crossing over and saturates near 50%, making it a systematic underestimate of physical distance over long intervals.
hint: Consider what happens to the order of alleles on a chromatid after two exchanges occur within the same interval.

q: Suppose a single crossover between genes P and Q occurred in 80% of meioses in a population of cells (and no other crossovers occurred). What recombination frequency would be observed between P and Q?
explain: A single crossover involves only 2 of the 4 chromatids, so each meiosis that has a crossover yields 2 recombinant and 2 parental chromatids, i.e. 50% recombinants. With crossovers in 80% of meioses, recombination frequency = 0.80 × 0.50 = 0.40 = 40%. (Even universal single crossovers cap RF at 50%.)
hint: Of the four chromatids in a meiosis with one crossover, how many are actually recombinant?

q: A student claims that two genes 70 map units apart on the same chromosome should show a recombination frequency of 70% in a testcross. What is the best correction to this claim?
options: The claim is correct because map distance equals RF at all distances
RF cannot exceed 50%, so these genes would show RF near 50% and appear unlinked despite being syntenic
RF would be exactly 35% because recombinants are always half the map distance
The genes would show 0% RF because they are too far apart to recombine
explain: Recombination frequency measured directly between two loci is capped at 50%, because as physical distance increases, multiple crossovers (especially even-numbered exchanges) restore parental allele combinations, driving the two genes toward independent assortment. Map distance is additive only when summed over many short intervals between intervening markers; it can exceed 50 units even though no single pairwise RF can. Therefore genes 70 map units apart show an observed RF near 50% and appear unlinked despite being syntenic, with their true distance recoverable only by adding RFs across intermediate markers.
hint: Ask whether a directly measured RF between two loci can keep climbing without bound, or whether something happens to crossovers once the genes are very far apart.

q: In a diploid fly heterozygous for a recessive eye-color allele, the recessive phenotype unexpectedly appears even though the second copy of the gene was inherited as wild type. The most likely explanation is that the wild-type homolog carries a deletion spanning that gene. What is this phenomenon called?
explain: When a deletion on one homolog removes the wild-type allele, the recessive allele on the intact homolog has no dominant counterpart to mask it and is therefore expressed phenotypically. This unmasking of a recessive allele by a hemizygosity-creating deletion is called pseudodominance. It is not blending (incomplete dominance), not joint expression of two alleles (codominance), and not one gene masking another (epistasis).
hint: Consider what happens to dominance when the opposing allele is physically absent rather than merely recessive.

q: A geneticist crosses flies carrying a known chromosomal deletion to flies homozygous for a recessive mutation m at an unmapped locus. If the deletion uncovers m (the offspring show the mutant phenotype), what can be concluded about the position of gene m?
options: Gene m lies within the segment removed by the deletion
Gene m lies outside the deleted segment
Gene m is on a different chromosome
Gene m has mutated into a dominant allele
explain: A deletion makes any gene in the missing segment hemizygous in the offspring, so a recessive allele on the intact homolog has no wild-type partner to mask it and is expressed (pseudodominance). The mutant phenotype therefore appears only if the deletion has physically removed the wild-type copy of that gene, meaning the locus lies within the deleted interval. This is the basis of deletion mapping, which assigns a gene to the defined region a deletion eliminates.
hint: Consider what must have happened to the normally-dominant wild-type copy for a single recessive allele to show up.

q: A recessive mutation r is tested against four overlapping deletions. Deletions Del-A and Del-B uncover r (mutant phenotype appears), while Del-C and Del-D do NOT uncover r (wild type). Which statement is best supported?
options: Gene r lies in a region removed by both Del-A and Del-B but retained by Del-C and Del-D
Gene r lies only in the region unique to Del-C
Gene r must lie outside all four deletions
Gene r is uncovered randomly and cannot be mapped
explain: A deletion uncovers a recessive allele (pseudodominance) only when it removes the corresponding wild-type gene, leaving the recessive allele hemizygous. Since Del-A and Del-B both produce the mutant phenotype, each must delete the r gene; since Del-C and Del-D give wild type, both retain a functional copy of r. Therefore r maps to the interval that is missing from both A and B yet still present in both C and D.
hint: For each deletion, ask whether the recessive phenotype showing up means the wild-type gene is present or absent, then find the interval common to the right set.

q: A student claims that observing pseudodominance proves the recessive mutation and the deletion are on the same chromosome arm but says nothing about which specific band the gene occupies. Using a panel of cytologically defined deletions with known breakpoints, why does pseudodominance actually give band-level resolution?
options: Each deletion's microscopically visible breakpoints define exact boundaries, so the smallest deleted interval that uncovers the allele pinpoints the band
Pseudodominance changes the recessive allele into a visible band on the chromosome
The recessive phenotype intensity is proportional to the gene's band number
Only the centromere position, not the breakpoints, sets the resolution
explain: Pseudodominance occurs when a recessive allele is uncovered because the homologous region is deleted, removing the masking wild-type copy. In a panel of cytologically mapped deletions, each deletion's breakpoints are visible at defined positions on the banded chromosome, so scoring which deletions do versus do not uncover the allele lets you intersect their deleted intervals. The gene must lie in the deletion segment common to all deletions that uncover it and absent from those that do not, and that smallest shared interval is bounded by visible breakpoints, yielding band-level localization. Resolution thus comes from how finely the breakpoints partition the region, not from phenotype intensity or centromere position.
hint: What do the known breakpoint positions of each deletion that uncovers the allele let you narrow down?

q: Seven recessive mutations (1-7) are tested against five deletions for pseudodominance. The 'uncovers' pattern places mutations into deletion intervals as follows: Del-I uncovers {1,2}; Del-II uncovers {2,3,4}; Del-III uncovers {4,5}; Del-IV uncovers {5,6}; Del-V uncovers {6,7}. Based on shared intervals, what is the correct linear gene order?
explain: Each deletion uncovers a contiguous block of loci, so every uncovered set must form an unbroken run in the true order, and the single mutation shared by two overlapping deletions marks where their intervals abut. Chaining {1,2}→{2,3,4}→{4,5}→{5,6}→{6,7} through their shared loci (2, 4, 5, 6) forces 3 to lie between 2 and 4 and fixes the line 1-2-3-4-5-6-7 (or its mirror image). Only one option keeps all five sets contiguous; every other arrangement breaks at least one deletion (e.g., splitting {4,5} or {2,3,4}), so the answer is uniquely determined.
hint: If each deletion removes a single continuous stretch, what must be true of the relative positions of all the mutations it uncovers?

q: During meiosis in an organism heterozygous for an inversion, how do the normal and inverted homologs achieve point-by-point pairing along their lengths?
options: By forming an inversion loop in one of the homologs
By remaining completely unpaired in the inverted region
By forming a synaptonemal bridge between two centromeres
By duplicating the inverted segment before synapsis
explain: In an inversion heterozygote one homolog carries a chromosomal segment in reversed (inverted) order relative to the other. For homologous loci to synapse point-by-point along their entire lengths, the chromosomes loop in the inverted region so that one strand twists back on itself, forming a characteristic inversion loop. Within this loop, each locus on the inverted segment can align with its corresponding locus on the normal homolog, allowing complete pairing despite the reversed sequence.
hint: If one homolog reads its genes in reverse order, what physical contortion would let each locus still face its true partner?

q: A single crossover occurs within the inversion loop of an individual heterozygous for a PARACENTRIC inversion. Which abnormal products result?
options: A duplication chromosome and a deletion chromosome
A dicentric bridge and an acentric fragment
Two monocentric chromosomes, each with a small duplication
A ring chromosome and a normal chromatid
explain: A paracentric inversion excludes the centromere (both breakpoints lie on one arm). A single crossover within the inversion loop in a heterozygote produces one recombinant chromatid carrying both centromeres (dicentric) and another recombinant piece with no centromere (acentric). At anaphase the dicentric stretches into a bridge between the poles and breaks, while the acentric fragment cannot attach to the spindle and is lost. In contrast, pericentric inversion crossovers yield duplication/deletion products, so that option is the distractor here.
hint: Recall whether a paracentric inversion includes the centromere, and tally how many centromeres each recombinant chromatid ends up with after one crossover in the loop.

q: A single crossover occurs within the inversion loop of an individual heterozygous for a PERICENTRIC inversion. What is the genetic makeup of the recombinant gametes?
options: They carry a dicentric chromosome and lose an acentric fragment
They are genetically normal and fully viable
They carry chromosomes with duplications and deletions
explain: A pericentric inversion spans the centromere, so a single crossover within the inversion loop yields recombinant chromatids that each retain exactly one centromere — no dicentric bridge or acentric fragment forms (those arise in paracentric inversions). Because the crossover occurs inside the loop, the recombinant chromatids end up duplicated for the regions on one side of the inversion and deleted for the regions on the other side, producing genetically unbalanced (dup/del) gametes.
hint: Where does the centromere sit relative to the inverted segment, and does that allow a bridge to form or force the imbalance to appear another way?

q: Inversions are often described as 'crossover suppressors' in heterozygotes. What does this phrase actually mean?
options: Crossing over is physically blocked from occurring within the loop
Recombinant crossover products are abnormal and rarely recovered in offspring
The inversion deletes the genes needed for recombination enzymes
Crossovers are shifted entirely outside the chromosome
explain: Crossing over physically does occur within the inversion loop in a heterozygote, so it is not blocked. However, a single crossover inside the loop produces unbalanced recombinant chromatids—dicentric and acentric in paracentric inversions, or duplication/deletion in pericentric ones—that are inviable or lethal. Because these recombinant products are not recovered among surviving progeny, recombination appears suppressed even though the crossover event itself happened.
hint: Ask whether "suppression" refers to the crossover event being prevented or to the recombinant offspring failing to survive and be counted.

q: A paracentric inversion heterozygote in a plant carries no missing or extra DNA within the inversion itself, yet its recombinant gametes are inviable. Why is the inversion heterozygote itself phenotypically normal despite producing abnormal gametes?
options: The inversion duplicates every affected gene to compensate
The inversion simply reorders genes without losing any genetic material
The dicentric bridge repairs itself before fertilization
Crossing over never occurs in the heterozygote
explain: An inversion flips the orientation of a chromosome segment but neither adds nor deletes any genes, so the heterozygote retains a complete, balanced gene complement and develops a normal phenotype. Abnormal products appear only at meiosis: a crossover within the inversion loop generates a dicentric bridge and an acentric fragment, producing unbalanced recombinant chromatids that yield inviable gametes. Thus normal somatic phenotype and defective recombinant gametes are fully consistent.
hint: Does rearranging the order of a segment change how many copies of each gene the cell actually contains?

q: A researcher studies a paracentric inversion heterozygote. In a normal (non-inversion) control cross, the two loci flanking the inverted region recombine at 22%. Assuming every single crossover within the inversion loop yields inviable gametes, what recombination frequency does she expect to RECOVER between those two loci in viable offspring of the inversion heterozygote?
options: Approximately 0%, because recombinant products are not recovered
11%, exactly half of the control value
explain: In a paracentric inversion heterozygote, any single crossover within the inversion loop generates a dicentric chromatid (bridge) and an acentric fragment; both are inviable, and these are precisely the products carrying the recombinant arrangement of the flanking loci. Because the recombinant gametes are selectively eliminated, almost none survive to be counted among viable offspring, so the recovered recombination frequency collapses toward 0% regardless of the 22% control value. This selective loss of recombinants is why inversions act as crossover suppressors.
hint: If the very gametes carrying a crossover are the ones that die, what fraction of recombinants remains among the survivors you can actually score?

q: At anaphase I in a paracentric inversion heterozygote, an observer sees a chromosome stretched between the two spindle poles and a small unattached piece of chromatin left near the metaphase plate. What are these two structures, respectively?
options: A pericentric duplication and a deletion loop
A dicentric bridge and an acentric fragment
A lagging univalent and a polar body
A normal bivalent and a recombination nodule
explain: A crossover within the loop of a paracentric inversion heterozygote yields one chromatid with two centromeres (dicentric) and one with none (acentric). At anaphase I the dicentric is pulled toward both poles simultaneously, stretching into a bridge, while the acentric fragment, lacking a centromere, cannot attach to spindle fibers and lags near the plate before being lost. This bridge-fragment configuration is diagnostic of paracentric inversion heterozygotes.
hint: Count how many centromeres end up on each recombinant product, and ask which one the spindle can actually grab from both sides.

q: In a pericentric inversion heterozygote, single crossovers occur within the inversion loop in 30% of meioses (each such meiosis gives 2 recombinant and 2 parental chromatids). If the recombinant (dup/del) gametes are all nonviable and meioses without a loop crossover give only parental gametes, what fraction of the TOTAL gametes produced is expected to be inviable?
explain: Only the 30% of meioses with a loop crossover produce any unbalanced gametes, and in each of those meioses just 2 of the 4 chromatids (half) are the dup/del recombinant products. Multiplying the fraction of affected meioses by the fraction of unbalanced chromatids per meiosis gives 0.30 × 0.50 = 0.15, so 15% of all gametes are inviable while the remaining 85% are balanced and viable.
hint: Weight the share of meioses that even have a crossover by the share of the four chromatids that come out unbalanced in those meioses.

q: A plant is heterozygous for a reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 3 and 7. During meiosis I, how many chromosomes participate in the cross-shaped pairing configuration formed at synapsis?
options: Two chromosomes pairing as a normal bivalent
Three chromosomes pairing as a trivalent
Four chromosomes pairing as a quadrivalent
Six chromosomes pairing as a hexavalent
explain: A reciprocal translocation heterozygote possesses two structurally normal chromosomes and two reciprocally translocated chromosomes, all four of which share homologous segments distributed across the rearrangement breakpoints. Because each segment must pair with its homolog, the four chromosomes synapse together into a single cross (cruciform) configuration, a quadrivalent. Thus four chromosomes participate in the cross-shaped pairing.
hint: Tally how many distinct chromosomes carry the swapped segments and must all line up homologous region to homologous region.

q: In a translocation heterozygote, which segregation pattern from the quadrivalent produces gametes that are genetically balanced and therefore viable?
explain: In a translocation heterozygote, the four chromosomes pair as a cross-shaped quadrivalent at meiosis. Only the segregation pattern in which chromosomes lying diagonally opposite in the cross move to the same pole sends one complete set (both normal chromosomes) to one gamete and the other complete reciprocal set (both translocated chromosomes) to the other. Because neither resulting gamete has any duplicated or deleted genetic material, both are balanced and viable; all other patterns (adjacent-1, adjacent-2, and 3:1) split the cross so that gametes carry overlapping/missing segments and are unbalanced.
hint: Picture the cross-shaped quadrivalent: which pairing of poles keeps each gamete's gene set complete and non-overlapping?

q: Why are the gametes produced by adjacent-1 segregation in a reciprocal-translocation heterozygote usually inviable?
options: They carry an extra full chromosome set, making them triploid
They each contain a duplication of one segment and a deletion of another
They lack a centromere and cannot move during anaphase
They contain only translocated chromosomes and no normal ones
explain: In a reciprocal-translocation heterozygote the four chromosomes pair in a cross-shaped quadrivalent. Adjacent-1 segregation sends homologous centromeres to opposite poles but pairs the wrong non-homologous chromosomes together, so each resulting gamete carries one normal and one translocated chromosome whose combined content duplicates one chromosomal segment and deletes another. This partial trisomy/monosomy creates a gene-dosage imbalance that typically makes the gamete or zygote inviable; the chromosomes still have normal centromeres and there is no extra full chromosome set.
hint: Consider what happens to gene dosage when neighboring, non-homologous chromosomes travel to the same pole instead of true homologs.

q: The reduced fertility observed in plants heterozygous for a reciprocal translocation, where roughly half of the gametes or offspring fail, is referred to as:
explain: In a reciprocal translocation heterozygote, meiotic pairing forms a cross-shaped configuration that segregates by alternate (balanced gametes) or adjacent (unbalanced duplication/deficiency gametes) patterns at roughly equal frequency. The unbalanced gametes from adjacent segregation are nonviable, so about half of the gametes or resulting offspring fail. This characteristic partial loss of fertility is called semisterility; heterosis, position effect, and pseudodominance describe unrelated genetic phenomena.
hint: What single term captures a heterozygote that loses about half of its functional gametes due to unbalanced segregation?

q: A reciprocal-translocation heterozygote is testcrossed and produces 480 fertilized ovules. Assuming only alternate segregation yields viable balanced gametes and adjacent segregation contributes equally but yields inviable gametes, approximately how many viable offspring are expected?
explain: In a reciprocal-translocation heterozygote, the meiotic quadrivalent segregates by alternate segregation (balanced, viable gametes) or adjacent segregation (unbalanced, inviable gametes), and these two classes occur at roughly equal frequency. This produces the classic semisterility: about half the gametes are balanced. Applying this to 480 fertilized ovules, approximately 480 × 1/2 = 240 viable offspring are expected.
hint: If balanced and unbalanced gametes arise in roughly equal proportions, what fraction of the ovules survives?

q: A geneticist crosses a translocation heterozygote and recovers many fewer semisterile-plus-balanced progeny than expected, finding instead an unusually high frequency of fully fertile (normal-karyotype) offspring. Which interpretation best explains a shift in segregation favoring viable, balanced gametes?
options: The quadrivalent is undergoing increased adjacent-2 segregation
Alternate segregation is occurring more frequently than adjacent segregation
Crossing over has been completely suppressed across the whole genome
The translocation has reverted, eliminating all rearranged chromosomes from gametes
explain: In a translocation heterozygote, the quadrivalent segregates via alternate (yielding balanced, viable gametes that are either fully normal or fully translocated) or adjacent-1/adjacent-2 (yielding unbalanced, inviable gametes). An excess of viable, balanced progeny over the expected semisterile baseline indicates a bias toward the alternate mode, which is the only mode producing balanced gametes. Increased adjacent segregation would raise the fraction of inviable unbalanced gametes and lower fertility, while crossover suppression or "reversion" do not convert unbalanced gametes into balanced ones.
hint: Recall which of the quadrivalent's segregation modes yields viable gametes versus the modes that produce inviable ones.

q: A newborn is diagnosed with Down syndrome. Which chromosomal change is responsible for this condition?
explain: Down syndrome is caused by an extra copy of chromosome 21, giving three copies (trisomy 21) instead of two. Monosomy 21 (a single copy) is a distinct, far more severe disorder, while trisomy 18 corresponds to Edwards syndrome and trisomy 13 to Patau syndrome.
hint: Which specific numbered chromosome carries an extra copy in this syndrome, and is the change a gain or a loss?

q: In the roughly 95% of Down syndrome cases classified as primary trisomy 21, what is the underlying mechanism producing the extra chromosome?
options: A reciprocal translocation between chromosomes 21 and 22
Nondisjunction during meiosis, most often in maternal meiosis I
Mitotic crossing over in the early embryo
explain: Primary (free) trisomy 21, accounting for roughly 95% of Down syndrome cases, results from nondisjunction—the failure of homologous chromosomes (or sister chromatids) to separate during gamete formation. This most commonly occurs in maternal meiosis I, yielding a gamete with two copies of chromosome 21 that, after fertilization, produces a zygote with three copies. Translocations and inversions are structural rearrangements that account for other, less common forms (e.g., translocation Down syndrome), not free trisomy.
hint: Ask whether the extra chromosome comes from a chromosome failing to separate or from a structural rearrangement of its arms.

q: Which factor shows the strongest, well-established correlation with the incidence of primary trisomy 21?
options: Number of prior pregnancies regardless of age
explain: The incidence of primary trisomy 21 from meiotic nondisjunction rises sharply and consistently with advancing maternal age, the most robustly documented risk factor, reflecting age-related errors in oocyte chromosome segregation. Paternal blood type, maternal height, and prior pregnancy count independent of age have no established correlation with nondisjunction frequency.
hint: Think about which gamete is arrested in meiosis for decades and whose contributing parent's age epidemiologists track most closely.

q: A phenotypically normal woman repeatedly has children with Down syndrome, and her own karyotype shows 45 chromosomes with a chromosome 21 fused to a chromosome 14. How is she best described?
options: She is a balanced carrier of a Robertsonian translocation
She carries an extra free chromosome 21
explain: A Robertsonian translocation fuses the long arms of two acrocentric chromosomes (here 14 and 21), yielding a karyotype of 45 chromosomes that nonetheless retains essentially all functional genetic material, since only the redundant rRNA-bearing short arms are lost; the carrier is therefore phenotypically normal and balanced. During meiosis, adjacent segregation can produce a gamete carrying the 14;21 fusion chromosome plus a normal chromosome 21, which upon fertilization with a normal gamete gives translocation Down syndrome offspring, explaining her recurrent affected children. She herself is neither trisomic (which would require 47 chromosomes) nor mosaic, and she carries no extra free chromosome 21.
hint: Count her chromosomes and ask whether any genetic material was actually gained or lost, only rearranged.

q: Two infants are born with severe, usually lethal autosomal trisomies. One has trisomy 13 and the other has trisomy 18. Which syndromes do they have, respectively?
explain: Trisomy 13 is Patau syndrome and trisomy 18 is Edwards syndrome, both severe and usually lethal in infancy. Down syndrome corresponds to trisomy 21 and is not represented here. Therefore the two infants have Patau syndrome and Edwards syndrome, respectively.
hint: Recall the named eponym tied to each specific chromosome number (13, 18, 21) and match them in order.

q: A genetic counselor distinguishes familial (translocation) Down syndrome from primary trisomy 21. Which statement correctly contrasts them?
options: Familial Down syndrome incidence rises with maternal age, while primary trisomy does not
Translocation Down syndrome can be inherited from a balanced carrier parent, while primary trisomy is not familial
Primary trisomy 21 patients carry only 45 chromosomes, while translocation patients carry 47
Translocation Down syndrome is more common than primary trisomy 21
explain: Translocation (familial) Down syndrome arises from an extra copy of chromosome 21 material fused to another chromosome (commonly a Robertsonian 14;21), which can be transmitted by a phenotypically normal balanced-carrier parent who has 45 chromosomes; it therefore recurs in families and is independent of maternal age. Primary trisomy 21 results from meiotic nondisjunction giving three free copies of chromosome 21 (47 total), is sporadic rather than inherited, accounts for about 95% of cases, and is the form whose incidence rises with maternal age. Thus only the statement that translocation Down syndrome can be inherited from a balanced carrier while primary trisomy is not familial is correct.
hint: Recall which form is sporadic and tied to maternal age versus which can run in families.

q: In a study population, 860 of 900 surveyed Down syndrome cases are primary nondisjunction trisomy 21 and the remainder are translocation cases. Approximately what percentage are translocation cases, and is this consistent with the expected breakdown?
options: About 4.4%, consistent with translocation being the minority (~5%) form
About 44%, indicating translocation is the majority form
About 95.6%, consistent with translocation being the common form
About 22%, consistent with roughly equal forms
explain: The translocation cases equal the total minus the primary nondisjunction cases: 900 - 860 = 40. As a fraction of the total, 40/900 = 0.0444, or about 4.4%. This matches the well-established clinical breakdown, in which free (primary nondisjunction) trisomy 21 accounts for roughly 95% of Down syndrome cases while translocation Down syndrome is the minority form at only a few percent.
hint: What is left over after removing the 860 primary cases from 900, and what share of the whole does that remainder represent?

q: A botanist doubles the entire chromosome set of a single dandelion species in cell culture, producing a tetraploid line entirely from that one species. This is best described as:
explain: Polyploidy arising from multiplication of chromosome sets all derived from a single species is autopolyploidy; doubling one dandelion species' genome to tetraploid fits this exactly. Allopolyploidy and amphidiploidy specifically require chromosome sets from two different species combined via hybridization, which did not occur here. Aneuploidy refers to gain or loss of individual chromosomes rather than entire genome doubling, so it is excluded.
hint: Consider how many distinct species contributed the duplicated chromosome sets, and whether whole sets or single chromosomes changed.

q: An autotriploid (3n) plant is almost always sterile. What is the underlying meiotic reason?
options: Triploids lack a spindle apparatus and cannot complete meiosis at all
Three homologs of each type cannot segregate evenly, producing chromosomally unbalanced gametes
The third set of chromosomes is always genetically defective
Triploid cells arrest before DNA replication and never enter meiosis
explain: In a triploid, each chromosome type is present as three homologs, which pair as trivalents (or a bivalent plus a univalent) at meiosis I. An odd number of homologs cannot be partitioned equally between the two spindle poles, so each chromosome segregates randomly (2:1 or otherwise) and independently. The result is aneuploid, chromosomally unbalanced gametes, and the probability of obtaining a complete balanced (euploid) set across all chromosomes is vanishingly small, causing near-complete sterility. Spindle assembly and DNA replication occur normally, so the defect is in segregation, not in entering or completing meiosis.
hint: Consider whether three copies of a chromosome can ever be divided equally between just two daughter poles.

q: Two related grass species, one with 2n = 14 and one with 2n = 18, are crossed. The F1 hybrid is robust but completely sterile. The most likely cause of this sterility is that:
options: The hybrid is haploid and therefore cannot reproduce
The two parental chromosome sets have no homologous partners to pair with at meiosis
The hybrid has too many chromosomes for the spindle to handle
explain: The F1 hybrid receives one haploid chromosome set from each parent (n=7 + n=9 = 16 chromosomes), so it is diploid, not haploid. Because the two parental sets come from different species, the chromosomes lack homologous partners and cannot synapse and segregate properly at meiosis, producing unbalanced, nonviable gametes. The plant can still be somatically vigorous because mitosis does not require pairing, which is why robustness and sterility coexist.
hint: For chromosomes to line up and separate cleanly at meiosis, what must each one be able to find first?

q: The sterile F1 hybrid from a cross of two species spontaneously doubles its chromosome number, becoming an amphidiploid (allotetraploid). Why is this amphidiploid typically FERTILE?
options: Doubling removes one of the two original parental genomes, restoring diploidy
Every chromosome now has a homologous partner, so meiosis pairs and segregates normally
Polyploids no longer require meiosis to make seeds
The extra sets cancel out the genetic differences between the two species
explain: In the sterile F1 hybrid, the single chromosome set from each parent species lacks homologous partners, so meiotic pairing fails and gametes are unbalanced. Chromosome doubling gives every chromosome an identical duplicate, so all chromosomes form bivalents and segregate evenly at meiosis, yielding balanced, viable gametes. Both parental genomes are retained (not removed or cancelled), and meiosis is still required to produce gametes/seeds.
hint: What does a chromosome need a partner for during the first meiotic division, and what did doubling just supply?

q: A fertile amphidiploid was created by doubling a sterile F1 hybrid between species X (2n = 12) and species Y (2n = 20). What is the somatic (2n) chromosome number of the amphidiploid?
explain: Species X contributes a haploid set of n = 6 and species Y a haploid set of n = 10, so the sterile F1 hybrid has 6 + 10 = 16 chromosomes (one of each kind, hence no pairing partners). Chromosome doubling of the amphidiploid produces a complete homologous partner for every chromosome, giving 16 x 2 = 32, so the fertile amphidiploid has a somatic number of 2n = 32.
hint: Build the hybrid from one gamete set of each parent, then ask what doubling does to that total.

q: Which of the following is an example of allopolyploidy rather than autopolyploidy?
options: A single species whose somatic cells spontaneously double to 4n
A 3n banana arising from a 2n x 4n cross within one species
A fertile crop derived from hybridizing two distinct wheat species and then doubling the chromosomes
A plant that becomes tetraploid after colchicine treatment of one species' tissue
explain: Allopolyploidy results from combining chromosome sets of two genetically distinct species via hybridization followed by chromosome doubling, whereas autopolyploidy multiplies chromosome sets derived from a single species. Only the fertile crop made by hybridizing two distinct wheat species and then doubling its chromosomes involves two different species, making it allopolyploid. Spontaneous somatic doubling, a 2n x 4n cross within one species, and colchicine-induced tetraploidy of one species' tissue all derive their extra sets from a single species and are therefore autopolyploid.
hint: Ask which scenario brings together genomes from more than one species rather than duplicating a single species' set.

q: Seedless watermelons are autotriploids produced by crossing a tetraploid (4n) with a diploid (2n) of the same species. Which statement best explains why these triploids are seedless yet still desirable to grow?
options: They are seedless because the triploid is a sterile interspecies hybrid lacking homologs entirely
They are seedless because three sets cannot segregate evenly into balanced gametes, but somatic growth of the fruit is unaffected
They are seedless because triploids cannot undergo mitosis, halting embryo formation
They are seedless because doubling to 4n first destroyed all the diploid's fertility permanently
explain: An autotriploid (3n) from a 4n x 2n cross within one species has three homolog sets that cannot segregate evenly at meiosis, yielding unbalanced (aneuploid) gametes and therefore no viable seeds—meiotic sterility. Mitosis is entirely normal, so somatic cell division builds the plant body and the fruit develops normally despite the sterility, making the seedless fruit desirable. The plant has homologs (not an interspecies hybrid), does undergo mitosis, and the tetraploid parent is fertile, ruling out the other options.
hint: Which kind of cell division produces gametes/seeds, and does having an odd number of chromosome sets disrupt that same process or the one that grows tissue?

q: During meiosis, two homologs misalign at a region of repeated DNA, and a crossover occurs between the mispaired segments. Which pair of products results from this single unequal crossover event?
options: One chromatid with a duplication and the reciprocal chromatid with a deletion
Two chromatids that both carry the same duplication
One chromatid with an inversion and one with a translocation
Two chromatids that are both completely wild type
explain: Unequal crossing over occurs when homologs misalign at repeated sequences so the crossover point is offset relative to the true alignment. Because recombination is reciprocal and DNA is conserved, the segment displaced from one chromatid is added to the other: one recombinant chromatid carries a duplication of the region while the reciprocal chromatid carries a corresponding deletion. Inversions and translocations arise from different mechanisms and are not products of a single unequal crossover.
hint: Recombination is reciprocal and conserves DNA—if one product picks up extra sequence, what must the partner product look like?

q: In Drosophila, the Bar phenotype (narrow, bar-shaped eye) arises from a tandem duplication of a chromosomal region. A fly carrying double-Bar has an even narrower eye with fewer facets than a single-Bar fly. This stronger effect with more copies is best described as a:
options: Loss-of-function mutation in a facet gene
Gene dosage effect of the duplicated region
Reversion of the duplication to wild type
explain: The Bar locus phenotype severity tracks the number of copies of the duplicated 16A region, not any change in the DNA sequence of a gene: double-Bar carries more copies than single-Bar and produces a narrower eye with fewer facets. Because phenotype intensity scales with copy number of an intact region, this is a gene dosage effect (the classic Drosophila example, arising from unequal crossing over). It is not a loss-of-function point mutation, a reversion, or a sex-linked epistatic interaction.
hint: Ask whether the gene's sequence is altered at all, or whether only the number of intact copies differs between the two genotypes.

q: Two arrangements of the Bar region give the SAME total number of copies but DIFFERENT phenotypes: a fly with both duplicated segments on one homolog shows a more severe eye reduction than a fly with one duplicated segment on each of the two homologs. This observation is best explained by a:
options: Gene dosage effect, since copy number differs between the two flies
Position effect, since phenotype depends on the arrangement of copies, not just their number
Maternal effect of the duplicated region
Failure of the duplication to be transcribed in either genotype
explain: Both flies carry the same total number of Bar duplication segments, so any explanation based purely on gene dosage (copy number) is excluded by the premise. The only difference is the spatial arrangement: both copies in cis on one homolog versus one copy on each homolog (trans). Because the more severe eye reduction tracks this arrangement rather than the count, the effect is a position effect—the classic cis/trans Bar demonstration. Maternal effect and a transcription failure are unsupported and would not produce an arrangement-dependent difference.
hint: Copy number is identical in both flies, so what else about the duplicated segments could differ?

q: Suppose wild-type flies average 780 eye facets, single-Bar flies average 360 facets, and double-Bar flies average 60 facets. Treating each added copy of the Bar region as one 'dose,' approximately how many facets are lost per added Bar dose when going from single-Bar (1 extra dose) to double-Bar (2 extra doses)?
options: About 300 facets lost per added dose
About 60 facets lost per added dose
About 420 facets lost per added dose
About 720 facets lost per added dose
explain: Single-Bar averages 360 facets and double-Bar averages 60 facets, a difference of 360 - 60 = 300 facets. These two genotypes differ by exactly one added Bar dose, so dividing the 300-facet loss by one dose yields about 300 facets lost per added dose, illustrating the copy-number-dependent dosage effect.
hint: Take the facet difference between the two genotypes and divide by how many doses separate them.

q: A female heterozygous for single-Bar can occasionally produce offspring carrying double-Bar AND offspring carrying a fully wild-type (round-eye) reverted chromosome. What single mechanism best accounts for the simultaneous appearance of both new chromosome types?
options: Independent point mutations occurring in two different gametes
Unequal crossing over between the tandemly repeated segments, producing reciprocal duplication and deletion products
Nondisjunction of the Bar-bearing chromosome during meiosis I
Gene conversion acting only on the Bar locus
explain: Bar is a tandem duplication, so in a heterozygote the repeated segments can mispair and a crossover between the misaligned copies yields reciprocal products in a single meiosis: one homolog gains an extra copy (double-Bar) while the other loses a copy, reverting to the wild-type round-eye arrangement. Only a reciprocal, copy-number-changing event explains the simultaneous appearance of both a duplication-gain and a deletion-loss. Point mutations in separate gametes are independent and not reciprocal, nondisjunction alters whole-chromosome number rather than locus copy number, and gene conversion is non-reciprocal so it cannot produce both products at once.
hint: Recall how mispairing of tandem repeats during meiosis can change copy number in two reciprocal ways at once.
